content.text: Enterprise AI programmes routinely attempt to run eight or more concurrent initiatives with a fixed pool of scarce specialists. Without a deliberate system for matching capacity to demand, the portfolio drifts into the same pathology every quarter: a handful of high-visibility projects consume the best people at 110% utilisation, lower-priority work stalls for want of a data scientist, and the platform team carries hidden debt because toil and maintenance were never counted in anyone's plan.
Resource allocation and balancing

is the ongoing practice that prevents this — defining what capacity exists, assigning it to initiatives in proportion to strategic value, monitoring utilisation in real time, and redistributing when priorities or workloads shift.
What It Is
Resource allocation is the process of deciding which human, computational, and financial assets are assigned to which AI initiatives, in what proportion, and for how long.
Resource balancing
is the complementary feedback loop: monitoring actual utilisation against planned utilisation and taking corrective action when the two diverge.
Together they operate across three dimensions. The
supply side

is the authorised headcount and infrastructure budget available to the AI organisation: ML engineers, data scientists, MLOps/platform engineers, product managers, domain subject-matter experts (SMEs), GPU/compute quotas, data storage, and financial budget. The
demand side
is the portfolio of active and incoming projects, each with its own resource profile and urgency. The
allocation mechanism
is the process — usually a quarterly planning event plus a continuous monitoring loop — that matches supply to demand and records the result in an allocation matrix that all stakeholders can read.
Resource allocation sits inside
programme management

within the enterprise AI governance layer. It is upstream of project scheduling (which assumes resources are already committed) and downstream of portfolio prioritisation (which decides which projects are approved). Its output is a living capacity plan that specifies, for each time period, what fraction of each resource type is assigned to each approved initiative.
Why It Matters
Scarcity is structural.

Senior ML engineers and data scientists with production experience are supply-constrained. A typical enterprise AI organisation has 15–40 engineers across a portfolio of 6–12 projects. Unmanaged allocation means the most vocal project managers capture the best people, not the highest-value projects.
Invisible over-allocation is the leading cause of AI project delays.

Individual contributors frequently agree to simultaneous commitments across multiple workstreams without a central system flagging the total. The result is context-switching overhead that industry research consistently places at 20–40% of lost capacity.
Compute costs compound without oversight.

GPU instances left running between experiments, or provisioned optimistically without a utilisation gate, quickly dwarf the human cost of a programme. Allocation discipline applied to compute prevents cloud bills from growing faster than delivered value.
Regulatory requirements demand auditability.

Governance frameworks including the EU AI Act, SOX IT controls, and internal model-risk policies require an organisation to demonstrate that critical AI systems receive adequate resourcing. An allocation matrix provides the audit trail.
Quarterly rebalancing surfaces hidden under-delivery risk.

When teams self-report capacity, chronic under-resourcing on projects that lack executive champions remains invisible until milestone slippage occurs. Formal allocation reviews force the issue into the open.
Benefits
Throughput increases.

Pooled allocation across the portfolio allows scarce specialists to shift to whichever project is most capacity-constrained, rather than being siloed in a dedicated team that may be idle at a given moment.
Burn-out risk decreases.

Capping knowledge-worker utilisation at 70–80% leaves time for deep work, documentation, learning, and incident response. Consistent 100% allocation is a leading indicator of attrition in competitive AI talent markets.
Strategic priorities are enforced by numbers, not by politics.
When each project's allocation is recorded and publicly visible, a project with a low strategic rank cannot silently consume resources that belong to higher-ranked work.
Surge capacity becomes manageable.

Retaining 20–30% slack across the portfolio allows the organisation to absorb urgent intake — a regulatory requirement, a competitive threat, a failed vendor — without permanently derailing planned programmes.
Financial forecasting accuracy improves.
Linking headcount allocation percentages to project budgets produces bottom-up cost estimates that match actual expenditure far more closely than top-down budget models.
Vendor and contractor decisions are grounded in data.
When an internal resource constraint is quantified (e.g., MLOps is at 92% average utilisation for two consecutive quarters), the case for a staff augmentation contract is defensible.
Trade-offs and Challenges
Allocation granularity versus overhead.
Tracking at 5% FTE increments produces accurate data but requires significant administrative effort. Teams typically settle on 10–25% increments for human resources and daily averages for compute.
T-shaped vs. deep-specialist models.

A pooled allocation model assumes individuals can flex across projects. In practice, domain-specific ML work (e.g., computer vision on proprietary sensor data) resists generalist assignment. Programme managers must distinguish fungible capacity from context-locked capacity.
The hoarding incentive.

Project leads who have suffered past resource cuts develop a rational incentive to request more headcount than they need as a buffer. Without a portfolio-level view that penalises sandbagging, allocation data becomes unreliable.
Allocation lag.

Moving an ML engineer from one project to another productively takes 2–4 weeks (knowledge transfer, context ramp-up, prior work closure). The allocation plan must account for this transition time; on-paper rebalancing does not instantly translate to on-ground throughput.
Compute allocation lags human rebalancing.

Cloud GPU reservations, reserved-instance commitments, and on-premises cluster schedules operate on their own calendars. A project rebalanced out of the portfolio in week 2 may still hold reserved compute through end-of-quarter.
Resistance from high-performers.

The individuals most valuable to the portfolio are also the most sought-after for ad-hoc assignments, executive demos, and knowledge-sharing. Without explicit protection of their allocation, their capacity erodes from the bottom up.
How It Works
Resource allocation operates as a
three-layer system

: an annual or bi-annual strategic planning layer sets aggregate headcount and budget targets per business domain; a quarterly planning layer translates those targets into per-project FTE commitments and compute budgets; and a continuous monitoring layer tracks actual utilisation weekly and triggers rebalancing when deviations exceed thresholds.
At the quarterly level, a
capacity plan

is built by enumerating every approved project and every available resource category. For each project-resource pair, a programme manager elicits a demand estimate — typically expressed as a percentage of one FTE or as GPU-hours per week — validated against the project's current phase and deliverable schedule. The sum across all projects for each resource type must not exceed the supply-side ceiling, adjusted downward by the
utilisation target
(usually 70–80% for knowledge workers, leaving 20–30% as managed slack).
When the raw demand sum exceeds available supply after applying the utilisation target, the programme management office convenes a
resource levelling
exercise. Resource levelling adjusts the scheduled start or duration of project phases to bring demand within supply, without changing the total scope of work. This is distinct from
resource smoothing

, which adjusts the resource profile within the project's float — moving work earlier or later within a phase's allowable window — without delaying the project end date. In AI portfolios, resource levelling is more common because project timelines are driven by data availability and model maturity, not by resource availability alone.

Between quarterly reviews, the monitoring layer compares planned allocation against timesheet actuals and infrastructure cost reports. Projects that exceed their planned utilisation by more than 10–15% for two consecutive weeks trigger a rebalancing event. The trigger is also fired by three external conditions: a project milestone completion (capacity becomes available), an executive priority change (demand shifts), or a new urgent project intake (new demand arrives that was not in the quarterly plan).
Architecture and Design
The allocation architecture comprises four artefacts: the
resource registry
, the
allocation matrix
, the
capacity plan document
, and the
utilisation dashboard
The
resource registry

is a maintained list of all individuals and infrastructure assets in scope, their role classifications, their available FTE or capacity units, and any constraints (e.g., domain lock, location, clearance level). It is the authoritative source of supply-side truth and is typically maintained by the AI CoE or PMO in a system such as Planview or a spreadsheet linked to the HRIS.
The
allocation matrix

maps projects on one axis against resource categories on the other, with time periods (sprints, months, quarters) as a third dimension. Each cell contains the planned allocation in percentage FTE or capacity units. The matrix is the primary artefact reviewed in quarterly planning sessions and rebalancing events.
The
capacity plan document

is a formal structured record of the allocation decisions made in a planning cycle: who decided, what was approved, what assumptions were made, and what the rebalancing triggers are. It is the governance artefact that satisfies audit requirements.
The
utilisation dashboard

aggregates timesheet data, Jira sprint reports, and cloud cost data to show actual versus planned utilisation for each resource type per project. Colour coding — green for 70–85%, amber for 55–70% and 85–95%, red outside these bands — enables rapid identification of imbalances.

attrs.alt: Resource Allocation and Balancing diagram: resource pool on the left feeding an allocation matrix in the centre, with capacity utilisation bars and rebalancing triggers on the right

content.text: When to Use It
Portfolio has three or more concurrent AI projects.
Below this threshold, ad-hoc coordination between project leads is usually sufficient. At three or more projects, hidden conflicts between resource demands become probable.
Scarcity exists in at least one critical role.
If data scientists or MLOps engineers are in short supply relative to demand, formal allocation prevents the most visible or politically connected projects from capturing all available capacity.
Project timelines span multiple quarters.
Short-lived initiatives (under 6 weeks) can be resourced opportunistically. Programmes that span two or more quarters require a maintained allocation plan to avoid drift.
Compute costs exceed $50K per month.
At this level, GPU and cloud storage allocation warrants the same rigour as human allocation. Ad-hoc provisioning without tracking produces compounding waste.
Organisation uses contractors or staff augmentation.
The decision to renew, extend, or terminate a contract engagement must be grounded in allocation data. Without it, contractor costs persist beyond their productive window.
Executive stakeholders demand resource transparency.
When a CTO or Chief Data Officer wants to understand how AI investment maps to business outcomes, an allocation matrix provides the traceability layer between headcount and initiative.
How to Implement
Step 1 — Build the resource registry.

Enumerate all roles in scope: ML engineers, data scientists, MLOps/platform engineers, product managers, domain SMEs. For each, record available FTE (typically 1.0 minus overhead for meetings, learning, and on-call, often netting to 0.8 effective FTE). Include infrastructure: GPU allocation in hours per week, cloud compute budget per month.
Step 2 — Define the allocation model.
Choose between three models:
FTE percentage allocation
(individual is 50% on Project A, 30% on Project B, 20% on platform);
pooled capacity
(capacity is assigned to a team pool and project leads draw from it); or
T-shaped teams

(a permanent cross-functional team with a stable core plus a flex ring). Most enterprise AI programmes use FTE percentage allocation for named senior contributors and pooled allocation for junior roles.
Step 3 — Establish the utilisation target.

Set 70–80% as the sustainable target for knowledge workers. Reserve 10% for unplanned work (incidents, urgent requests, technical debt). Reserve the remaining 10–20% as learning and development time. For compute, targets are higher (80–90%) because context-switching costs do not apply, but leave margin for experiment spikes.
Step 4 — Populate the initial allocation matrix.

For each approved project, gather demand estimates from the project lead. Validate against the project's current phase gate plan. Sum across projects per resource type. If the sum exceeds supply × utilisation target, apply resource levelling — delay lower-priority project phases until capacity is available.
Step 5 — Encode the plan in a YAML capacity document.
Structure the plan as a machine-readable file (see example below) so it can be diff’d across planning cycles and ingested by tooling for automated over-allocation checks.
Step 6 — Deploy a utilisation monitoring loop.

Connect timesheet data (Jira, Harvest, or similar) and cloud cost reports (AWS Cost Explorer, GCP Billing) to a dashboard. Set automated alerts for any role breaching the 85% threshold for more than two consecutive weeks.
Step 7 — Run quarterly rebalancing reviews.

Two weeks before quarter end, the PMO pulls the utilisation dashboard, identifies drift, and prepares a proposed rebalanced allocation matrix. A 90-minute session with project leads and the AI programme manager reconciles competing demands and produces the next quarter's plan.
Step 8 — Document rebalancing decisions.

Record the rationale for each material change (>10% FTE shift for any individual) in the capacity plan document. This audit trail is required for governance reviews and for onboarding new programme managers.

# capacity_plan_q3_2026.yaml
# Schema: projects x resource_types x period
# Utilisation target: 75% sustainable, 85% surge ceiling

metadata:
  version: "2026-Q3"
  owner: "AI Programme Office"
  approved_by: "Chief Data Officer"
  review_date: "2026-09-15"
  next_review: "2026-12-01"

resource_pool:
  ml_engineers:
    total_fte: 18
    effective_fte: 14.4          # 18 * 0.80 overhead factor
    utilisation_target: 0.75
    available_fte: 10.8          # 14.4 * 0.75

  data_scientists:
    total_fte: 10
    effective_fte: 8.0
    utilisation_target: 0.75
    available_fte: 6.0

  mlops_platform:
    total_fte: 6
    effective_fte: 4.8
    utilisation_target: 0.80     # platform roles tolerate slightly higher
    available_fte: 3.84

  product_managers:
    total_fte: 4
    effective_fte: 3.2
    utilisation_target: 0.70
    available_fte: 2.24

  domain_smes:
    total_fte: 8
    effective_fte: 6.4
    utilisation_target: 0.50     # SMEs carry primary job; AI is secondary
    available_fte: 3.2

  gpu_compute:
    weekly_hours: 240
    utilisation_target: 0.85
    available_hours: 204

  annual_budget_usd: 4_200_000

projects:
  - id: rec-engine
    name: "Recommendation Engine v2"
    priority: 1
    phase: "model-development"
    allocations:
      ml_engineers_fte: 4.0
      data_scientists_fte: 2.0
      mlops_platform_fte: 1.0
      product_managers_fte: 1.0
      domain_smes_fte: 0.5
      gpu_hours_per_week: 60

  - id: fraud-detection
    name: "Real-Time Fraud Detection"
    priority: 2
    phase: "production-hardening"
    allocations:
      ml_engineers_fte: 3.0
      data_scientists_fte: 1.5
      mlops_platform_fte: 1.5
      product_managers_fte: 0.5
      domain_smes_fte: 1.0
      gpu_hours_per_week: 40

  - id: nlp-pipeline
    name: "Customer Service NLP Pipeline"
    priority: 3
    phase: "feature-engineering"
    allocations:
      ml_engineers_fte: 2.0
      data_scientists_fte: 1.5
      mlops_platform_fte: 0.5
      product_managers_fte: 0.5
      domain_smes_fte: 1.0
      gpu_hours_per_week: 30

rebalancing_triggers:
  over_utilisation_threshold: 0.85
  over_utilisation_consecutive_weeks: 2
  under_utilisation_threshold: 0.55
  under_utilisation_consecutive_weeks: 2
  milestone_completion: true
  unplanned_intake_gate: "programme_manager_approval"
Examples
The following Python script reads a capacity plan YAML, computes per-role utilisation, and flags over-allocated and under-allocated roles with suggested rebalancing actions.

import yaml
from dataclasses import dataclass
from typing import List

@dataclass
class AllocationReport:
    role: str
    allocated: float
    available: float
    utilisation_pct: float
    status: str
    suggestion: str

def load_plan(path: str) -> dict:
    with open(path) as f:
        return yaml.safe_load(f)

def compute_utilisation(plan: dict) -> List[AllocationReport]:
    pool = plan["resource_pool"]
    projects = plan["projects"]
    reports = []

    human_roles = {
        "ml_engineers": "ml_engineers_fte",
        "data_scientists": "data_scientists_fte",
        "mlops_platform": "mlops_platform_fte",
        "product_managers": "product_managers_fte",
        "domain_smes": "domain_smes_fte",
    }

    for role_key, alloc_key in human_roles.items():
        if role_key not in pool:
            continue
        resource = pool[role_key]
        target = resource["utilisation_target"]
        effective = resource["effective_fte"]
        available = resource["available_fte"]

        allocated = sum(p["allocations"].get(alloc_key, 0.0) for p in projects)
        util = allocated / effective
        over_thresh = target + 0.10
        under_thresh = target - 0.20

        if util > over_thresh:
            status = "over"
            suggestion = (
                f"Reduce by {(util - target) * effective:.1f} FTE or add contractor."
            )
        elif util < under_thresh:
            status = "under"
            suggestion = (
                f"{(target - util) * effective:.1f} FTE slack. Pull in backlog or reduce contractors."
            )
        else:
            status = "healthy"
            suggestion = "No action required."

        reports.append(AllocationReport(
            role=role_key,
            allocated=round(allocated, 2),
            available=round(available, 2),
            utilisation_pct=round(util * 100, 1),
            status=status,
            suggestion=suggestion,
        ))

    gpu = pool.get("gpu_compute", {})
    if gpu:
        weekly = gpu["weekly_hours"]
        target = gpu["utilisation_target"]
        allocated_hrs = sum(p["allocations"].get("gpu_hours_per_week", 0) for p in projects)
        util = allocated_hrs / weekly
        over_thresh = target + 0.10
        under_thresh = target - 0.20

        if util > over_thresh:
            status = "over"
            suggestion = f"Over by {allocated_hrs - weekly * target:.0f} hrs/wk. Add spot instances."
        elif util < under_thresh:
            status = "under"
            suggestion = f"{weekly * target - allocated_hrs:.0f} hrs/wk available. Pull in experiments."
        else:
            status = "healthy"
            suggestion = "GPU within target band."

        reports.append(AllocationReport(
            role="gpu_compute",
            allocated=allocated_hrs,
            available=round(weekly * target),
            utilisation_pct=round(util * 100, 1),
            status=status,
            suggestion=suggestion,
        ))
    return reports

def print_report(reports: List[AllocationReport]) -> None:
    header = f"{'Role':<22} {'Alloc':>8} {'Avail':>8} {'Util%':>7}  Status    Suggestion"
    print(header)
    print("-" * 100)
    for r in reports:
        marker = "!" if r.status == "over" else ("v" if r.status == "under" else "+")
        print(f"{r.role:<22} {r.allocated:>8.1f} {r.available:>8.1f} {r.utilisation_pct:>6.1f}%  {marker} {r.status:<8} {r.suggestion[:55]}")

if __name__ == "__main__":
    plan = load_plan("capacity_plan_q3_2026.yaml")
    reports = compute_utilisation(plan)
    print_report(reports)
Real-World Example

A mid-size retail company operates eight concurrent AI initiatives with a team of 40 engineers distributed across ML engineering (22), data science (10), and MLOps/platform (8). Historically, quarterly planning was conducted in a single all-hands meeting that produced a spreadsheet circulated by email. By mid-quarter, three projects had informally negotiated extra capacity from the MLOps team without updating the spreadsheet, and the official allocation had diverged from reality by over 30%.

At the start of Q2, the newly appointed AI programme manager introduced a formal allocation process. A YAML-based capacity plan was drafted for all eight projects. The initial analysis revealed that the fraud detection and recommendation engine projects together accounted for 78% of all ML engineer demand — leaving the remaining six projects in a queue for the residual 22%. Three projects were, in the programme manager's words,
structurally under-resourced
: each had a data scientist at 0.5 FTE when their phase (feature engineering for complex datasets) required at least 1.5 FTE to make meaningful weekly progress.

The rebalancing action taken was threefold. First, the recommendation engine's ML engineering allocation was reduced from 6 FTE to 4 FTE, reflecting that the project had entered hyperparameter tuning — a phase dominated by compute, not human iteration. The 2 freed FTEs were redistributed across the three under-resourced projects. Second, one data scientist contract was extended by one quarter to cover the remaining shortfall. Third, the fraud detection project's MLOps allocation was increased from 1 FTE to 1.5 FTE, funded by reducing the recommendation engine's MLOps share (which had just completed its CI/CD pipeline build and needed only maintenance capacity).

The outcome after one quarter: the three previously under-resourced projects each delivered their planned phase gate, the fraud detection project went live on schedule, and the programme manager reported that the MLOps team's average utilisation had dropped from 97% to 81% — within the sustainable target band. Attrition risk in that team was assessed as reduced.
Common Mistakes
Planning at 100% utilisation.

Allocating every FTE to a project leaves no capacity for unplanned requests, on-call incidents, technical interviews, knowledge transfer, or learning. The system enters a permanently overloaded state; any disruption triggers cascading delays.
Hoarding scarce ML talent.

Project leads who have experienced past resource shortfalls request larger allocations than they need as a buffer, inflating demand. This depresses availability for other projects and produces allocation plans that do not reflect real work. Counter this with a portfolio-visible allocation matrix and a norm that unused allocations are relinquished monthly.
Ignoring toil and maintenance work.

Production ML systems require ongoing monitoring, data drift detection, model retraining, and incident response. These activities are rarely included in project-phase estimates. When maintenance volume grows with portfolio scale, it silently consumes the capacity nominally assigned to new development.
Treating domain SME time as free.

Subject-matter experts (underwriters, clinicians, logistics planners) are typically the hardest resource to secure, because they hold a primary job. Assuming their participation requires no allocation planning leads to projects that stall at feature validation and labelling stages.
Failing to account for ramp-up time.

When a rebalancing decision moves an engineer from one project to another, productivity does not transfer instantly. A 4-week ramp-up period should be modelled as reduced effective capacity in the destination project during that window.
Separating compute allocation from human allocation.

GPU and cloud compute plans built in isolation from headcount plans produce mismatches: models ready to train but no compute reserved, or compute reserved but no engineer available to run experiments. The capacity plan must cover both dimensions in the same document.
Point-in-time planning without monitoring.

A quarterly plan that is filed and not revisited until the next quarter loses its value within weeks as realities change. The plan is only effective if paired with a weekly or bi-weekly utilisation check against actuals.
Best Practices
Maintain a single source of truth for the allocation matrix.

All changes — approved, proposed, or superseded — should be version-controlled in a shared system (Planview, Smartsheet, or a Git-tracked YAML). Emailed spreadsheets create conflicting versions within days.
Distinguish fungible from context-locked capacity.

Label each role as fungible (can move between projects with 2–4 week ramp) or context-locked (requires domain knowledge that takes months to build). Context-locked resources require longer planning horizons and earlier reallocation decisions.
Set explicit over-utilisation alerts.
Configure your utilisation dashboard to send notifications when any role exceeds 85% for more than two consecutive weeks. Do not wait for quarterly reviews to surface sustained over-allocation.
Reserve 10% of allocation for platform and tooling work.

MLOps, feature store improvements, CI/CD for ML, and observability tooling are strategic investments that reduce future toil. Without a protected allocation, this work is perpetually deferred in favour of project delivery.
Apply the T-shaped team model for cross-project roles.

MLOps and data engineers who serve multiple projects should be organised in a pooled team with a product manager who maintains the demand queue. This prevents individual engineers from being context-switched by four different project leads simultaneously.
Build allocation decisions into project approval gates.
No project should receive approval without a confirmed resource commitment from the PMO. Approving projects without committed capacity creates an unfunded mandate that poisons the allocation system.
Model allocation in rolling 12-week windows.

Quarterly plans are necessary for budget management, but the allocation matrix should be maintained in 12-week rolling windows, updated monthly. This provides enough forward visibility to make staffing decisions without the false precision of annual plans.
Conduct a blameless utilisation retrospective each quarter.
Review which projects consumed more or less than planned, why, and what the allocation system failed to predict. Use these findings to improve demand estimation accuracy in the next cycle.
Performance and Scalability

The administrative overhead of resource allocation scales primarily with the number of projects and the number of distinct role types tracked. Below ten projects and five role categories, a maintained spreadsheet or Smartsheet template is adequate. As the portfolio grows to 15–20 projects with 8+ role types, the manual maintenance burden becomes prohibitive and dedicated resource management tooling is required.

Automated data integration is the key performance lever. Connecting the allocation matrix to live timesheet data (via Jira, Harvest, or Toggl APIs), Git commit activity, and cloud billing APIs allows the utilisation dashboard to update daily without manual data entry. The programme manager's role shifts from data collection to interpretation and decision-making.

For very large AI organisations (50+ engineers, 20+ concurrent projects), allocation planning requires hierarchical decomposition: a portfolio-level plan at the programme manager layer allocates capacity to programmes; each programme lead allocates their programme's capacity to individual projects. The two layers are reconciled monthly. Tools such as Planview Enterprise One and SAP Project and Resource Management are designed for this hierarchical model.

Compute allocation in large organisations requires integration with the cloud provider's reservation and commitment APIs. AWS Savings Plans and Reserved Instances, GCP Committed Use Discounts, and Azure Reserved Virtual Machine Instances all operate on their own planning horizons (1–3 years for the best discounts). The compute allocation plan must align with these commitment windows or absorb higher on-demand pricing for agility.
Tooling and Ecosystem
Resource management tooling falls into three categories:
portfolio and resource planning platforms
project management tools with resource features
, and
custom allocation dashboards
built on top of existing data infrastructure.
Portfolio and resource planning platforms
are purpose-built for the allocation use case.
Planview Enterprise One
and
Planview Portfolios
(formerly LeanKit) offer capacity modelling, demand forecasting, and role-based allocation across large project portfolios.
Smartsheet Resource Management
(formerly 10,000ft) is widely used in mid-size technology organisations for visual capacity heat maps and percentage-based allocation.
Resource Guru
provides a lightweight scheduling interface suitable for teams of 10–50. For organisations already deep in Microsoft tooling,
Microsoft Project Online
and
Project for the Web
(with Power BI integration) offer resource levelling and allocation reporting within the Microsoft 365 ecosystem.
Project management tools with resource features.
Jira Plans

(formerly Portfolio for Jira) enables capacity planning at the team level and can be used for basic allocation tracking when Jira is the primary project management system. Its resource model operates at the sprint-team level rather than the FTE-percentage level, which suits Agile delivery models but is less suited to cross-project portfolio analysis.
Linear
and
Shortcut
offer capacity tracking features but are oriented toward product engineering rather than programme-level AI delivery.
Custom dashboards.

Many AI organisations build allocation dashboards on top of their existing data stack. A common pattern: YAML capacity plan in Git as the source of truth, a Python ETL job that joins planned allocation against Jira time-tracking data and AWS Cost Explorer API output, and a Looker or Metabase dashboard surfacing per-role utilisation by project. This approach is more work to build but produces exact integration with the organisation's specific role taxonomy and project structure.
Comparison with Alternatives
Model
Description
Best Fit
Weaknesses
Project-based allocation
Each project is assigned a dedicated team for its duration. Resources are locked to the project until completion or explicit release.
Long-horizon, stable-scope projects; regulated industries requiring dedicated accountability
Idle capacity between phases; scarce specialists trapped in one project; high context-switch cost at project transitions
Pooled / shared services
Specialists sit in a centralised pool and are assigned to projects on demand, returning to the pool when work is complete.
Portfolios with variable project timelines; MLOps and platform engineering; data science at early stages
Context-switching overhead; relationship-building lag; no single project owns the specialist's priority queue
Product-team permanent assignment
A stable cross-functional team is assigned permanently to a product domain rather than to a project.
Mature AI products with continuous improvement cycles; recommendation engines, fraud models, search ranking
Team may be idle between cycles; harder to redirect to a new domain; higher headcount cost
Guild / chapter model
Specialists belong to a functional chapter that maintains standards and career development, while embedded in cross-functional product teams.
Organisations running scaled Agile frameworks; combines functional depth with product ownership
Dual reporting creates allocation ambiguity; chapter lead must negotiate resource splits with product leaders
FTE percentage allocation (this topic)
Individuals are allocated by percentage of their time across multiple projects, tracked in a matrix.
Mid-size AI programmes (3–12 projects, 15–50 engineers); organisations in transition from project-based to product-based models
Context-switching cost for high-percentage multi-project assignments; matrix maintenance overhead; individual priority confusion
Security and Reliability

The capacity plan and allocation matrix contain sensitive information: headcount by name, compensation-adjacent FTE data, strategic project names, and budget figures. Access controls should restrict the full allocation matrix to programme managers, the AI programme lead, finance business partners, and executive sponsors. Project leads should see only the rows pertaining to their own project.

Version control of the capacity plan in a private Git repository provides an immutable audit trail of changes, with author and timestamp. This is directly relevant to AI governance requirements: when a model risk review asks "was the fraud detection model adequately resourced during its development phase?", a git-blamed capacity plan YAML provides a defensible, timestamped answer.

Reliability of the allocation process itself depends on data quality in the connected systems. If engineers do not log time in Jira or the timesheet tool, the utilisation dashboard reflects planned rather than actual allocation and ceases to function as an early-warning system. Establishing a lightweight time-logging norm — 15 minutes per day of Jira ticket updates is sufficient for allocation-level accuracy — is the most impactful reliability investment in the process.
For compute allocation,
budget alerts

configured in the cloud provider's billing console provide a parallel reliability layer: if actual GPU spend exceeds 110% of the planned allocation for a project, an alert fires. This catches cases where engineers run ad-hoc experiments outside the planned compute budget — a common source of allocation drift that is invisible in the human-tracking layer.
Mental Model
Think of resource allocation as
traffic flow management on a city road network

. The roads are your engineers and infrastructure; the vehicles are project workstreams; throughput is the value delivered per unit of time. A naive traffic manager fills every road to 100% capacity. But a road operating at 100% capacity enters
stop-and-go congestion

— average throughput falls below what is achievable at 75% because incidents, merges, and variability create queues that propagate backward through the network. The optimal throughput on a knowledge-work road is achieved not by maximising utilisation but by maintaining a
20–30% capacity margin

that absorbs variability, allows fast context-switching when priorities change, and prevents the compounding delays of a fully saturated system. Rebalancing is the equivalent of traffic signal coordination: redistributing flow before any single route becomes a bottleneck, so the network as a whole maintains velocity.
Key Takeaways
Resource allocation is a portfolio function, not a project function.
Individual project managers optimise locally; only a portfolio-level view can optimise globally across competing demands for scarce AI talent and compute.
The sustainable utilisation target for knowledge workers is 70–80%.
Allocating to 100% is not a sign of efficiency — it is a source of compounding delay, burnout, and attrition.
The allocation matrix is the primary governance artefact.

A version-controlled, regularly maintained matrix that maps projects, resources, and time periods provides the audit trail required for AI governance and the decision support required for rebalancing.
Encode the capacity plan as a machine-readable YAML.
A YAML capacity plan enables automated over-allocation checks, diff-based change tracking, and integration with utilisation dashboards — none of which are achievable with a spreadsheet alone.
Rebalancing triggers must be explicit and acted on within two weeks.

Utilisation breaches, milestone completions, priority changes, and new urgent intake are the four canonical triggers. Delayed rebalancing compounds the resource mismatch that the trigger was designed to surface.
Maintenance and toil must be explicitly allocated.

As the production AI portfolio grows, so does the invisible maintenance burden. Reserving 10–15% of MLOps and ML engineer capacity for maintenance prevents production reliability degradation and reduces on-call burden on the project teams.
Tool choice should match portfolio scale.

A Git-tracked YAML plus a Metabase dashboard is adequate for 5–10 projects. Planview or Smartsheet Resource Management is appropriate for 15–30 projects. Do not over-invest in tooling before the process is established.
Start with a pilot on the most contested role.

Implement allocation tracking first for the scarcest resource in the portfolio — typically senior ML engineers or data scientists. Demonstrating value there builds the organisational habit before extending to all roles.
